mcqs.question: Thrashing occurs when:

options.text: System spends more time in page I/O than executing processes
Processes run in contiguous memory locations

mcqs.explanation: Thrashing is defined as the condition when the system spends most of its time swapping pages rather than executing instructions, causing severe performance degradation.

mcqs.question: Thrashing is caused by:

options.text: Insufficient frames leading to excessive page faults

mcqs.explanation: When processes don't have enough memory frames, they generate excessive page faults, and the constant swapping of pages in and out causes thrashing.

mcqs.question: The working set of a process is:

options.text: The total memory used by the process
The set of pages currently in memory
The set of pages the process has referenced in the recent past

mcqs.explanation: The working set is the set of pages that a process has referenced in a certain time interval (the window). It represents the pages the process actively needs.

mcqs.question: What happens to CPU utilization during thrashing?
mcqs.explanation: During thrashing, the CPU is idle waiting for page I/O operations, so utilization drops significantly from its optimal point.

mcqs.question: Which technique monitors page fault rate to control thrashing?
mcqs.explanation: PFF control directly monitors the page fault frequency of each process and adjusts frame allocation to prevent thrashing.

mcqs.question: In the working set model, thrashing occurs when:

options.text: Working set size is less than allocated frames
Working set size exceeds allocated frames

mcqs.explanation: When a process's working set (pages it actively needs) is larger than its allocated frames, it constantly faults and thrashes.

mcqs.question: Process suspension is a thrashing control technique that involves:

options.text: Swapping out entire processes to disk

mcqs.explanation: Suspension involves completely removing a process from memory (swapping to disk) to free all its frames for other processes.

mcqs.question: The page fault frequency curve shows:

options.text: CPU utilization vs. degree of multiprogramming

mcqs.explanation: The PFF curve plots CPU utilization against the degree of multiprogramming, showing the thrashing region where performance drops.

mcqs.question: What is prepaging?

options.text: Loading pages after a page fault occurs
Loading pages before they are needed based on prediction

mcqs.explanation: Prepaging brings pages into memory in advance, based on predictions, to reduce page faults when a suspended process resumes.

mcqs.question: In local page replacement:

options.text: Any process can replace any page
A process can only replace its own pages

mcqs.explanation: Local replacement restricts each process to replace only pages from its own allocated frames, preventing interference between processes.

mcqs.question: Global page replacement allows:

options.text: Only the active process to replace pages
Any process to replace any page in the system
Pages to be replaced in FIFO order only

mcqs.explanation: Global replacement gives all processes access to the entire pool of free frames, offering more flexibility but less isolation.

mcqs.question: The optimal degree of multiprogramming:

options.text: Maximizes CPU utilization before thrashing begins

mcqs.explanation: The optimal degree of multiprogramming is the point just before thrashing begins, maximizing CPU utilization.

mcqs.question: Which is NOT a method to control thrashing?
mcqs.explanation: Increasing CPU clock speed doesn't address the root cause of thrashing (insufficient memory frames). The other three are actual thrashing control techniques.

mcqs.question: When a process thrashes, it:

options.text: Spends most time in page fault handling

mcqs.explanation: A thrashing process constantly generates page faults and spends most of its time waiting for pages to be brought in from disk.

mcqs.question: Increasing the number of frames allocated to a process can:

options.text: Reduce its page fault rate and stop thrashing

mcqs.explanation: More frames mean the process can hold more of its working set in memory, reducing page faults and eliminating thrashing.

mcqs.question: Virtual memory thrashing occurs when:

options.text: Demand for pages exceeds physical memory capacity
There is too much cache memory
All pages are already in memory

mcqs.explanation: Virtual memory thrashing happens when the combined demand for pages from all processes exceeds the available physical memory.

mcqs.question: The relationship between CPU utilization and thrashing is:
mcqs.explanation: CPU utilization increases with more processes up to a point, then decreases inversely as thrashing begins and worsens.

mcqs.question: Thrashing primarily affects which aspect of system performance?
mcqs.explanation: Thrashing severely degrades throughput (amount of work done) and response time (how quickly tasks complete), making the system unusable.

mcqs.question: A small page size helps in reducing:

options.text: Both internal fragmentation and I/O time

mcqs.explanation: Smaller pages reduce internal fragmentation and I/O time per fault, but may increase page table size - a trade-off exists.

mcqs.text: Which replacement strategy can lead to thrashing if misused?
mcqs.explanation: Global replacement, while flexible, can allow one process to take too many frames, causing other processes to thrash.
mcqs.question: Which replacement strategy can lead to thrashing if misused?
